common_name: Cigar Calathea (Pampano, Cuban Cigar Plant, Wax Calathea)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or container-grown clumps in late spring in partial shade to full sun with consistently moist, rich, well-drained soil; Calathea lutea is one of the largest and most robust calatheas, reaching 6–10 feet tall — provide ample space. In zone 9, it grows as a large-leaf accent with rhizomes surviving mild winters under heavy mulch. The large paddle-shaped leaves are used to wrap food in tropical cuisine (similar to banana leaves). The white, powdery wax coating on the leaf undersides is characteristic. Propagate by rhizome division.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in consistently moist, organically rich, well-draining soil — Cigar Calathea grows vigorously in partial shade but also tolerates full sun in moist soils, and produces its largest leaves with adequate moisture and warmth. Calathea lutea is a large, clump-forming, rhizomatous tropical perennial native to Mexico, Central America, the Caribbean, and northern South America in the Marantaceae family — one of the largest-growing calatheas, reaching 6–10 feet in height, and one of the most useful and dramatic as a landscape accent in tropical and subtropical gardens. The leaves are enormous (up to 5–6 feet long, 18–24 inches wide), paddle-shaped, borne on tall, erect petioles, and characterized by a distinctive white, powdery, waxy bloom (pruinose coating) on the undersides that gives the leaf a two-tone appearance — bright green above, silvery-white below. The inflorescences that give the plant its common name 'Cigar Calathea' are compact, cylindrical, waxy yellow-green structures resembling a half-smoked cigar, emerging from near the base of the plant. The large leaves have been traditionally used throughout the Caribbean and Central America for wrapping food (particularly tamales), for thatching in Cuba ('pampano'), and as natural platters. In zone 9-11 gardens, Cigar Calathea creates an instant tropical landscape statement with its enormous leaves.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Calathea lutea for Florida tropical landscapes
LSU AgCenter — Large Tropical Foliage Plants for Gulf Coast Louisiana
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Calathea lutea for zone 9-10 tropical gardens
Missouri Botanical Garden — Calathea lutea species profile

common_name: Rattlesnake Ginger (Yellow Rattle Calathea, Rattlesnake Plant, Yellow Wax Calathea)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or container-grown clumps in late spring in partial shade with consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Calathea crotalifera prefers partial shade but tolerates full shade — it grows naturally as an understory plant in humid tropical forests. In zone 9, treat as a container plant moved indoors in winter as the plant cannot tolerate frost. The distinctive yellow, waxy, overlapping-bract inflorescences are excellent cut flowers (2–3 week vase life). Propagate by rhizome division. Non-toxic.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Rattlesnake Ginger produces its best foliage and inflorescence display in the bright, filtered light of a shaded tropical garden position or sheltered patio. Calathea crotalifera is a clump-forming, rhizomatous tropical perennial native to Mexico, Central America, and northern South America in the Marantaceae family, grown for its remarkable, novelty inflorescences as much as for its large, paddle-shaped foliage. The inflorescences are 8–14 inches long, erect to arching, and composed of tightly overlapping, waxy, bright yellow to golden-yellow bracts stacked in a precise, shingle-like pattern along the central axis — the bract arrangement, when viewed from the side, closely resembles the segmented rattle of a rattlesnake, explaining the common name 'Rattlesnake Plant.' From between the bracts, small white to pale yellow flowers emerge in succession. The yellow, waxy inflorescences are long-lasting (several weeks on the plant and 2–3 weeks as cut flowers) and have commercial value in the tropical cut flower industry. The large, green, paddle-shaped leaves provide an attractive, lush background to the colorful inflorescences. Multiple inflorescences can be produced simultaneously by established clumps through the warm months. An attractive zone 10-11 shade garden plant and excellent tropical cut flower.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Calathea crotalifera for Florida tropical landscapes
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Calathea crotalifera cut flower notes
Heliconia Society International — Marantaceae ornamentals for tropical gardens
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Foliage Ornamentals for Gulf Coast Louisiana Zone 10

common_name: Christmas Heliconia (Holiday Heliconia, Narrow Heliconia, Angusta Heliconia)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or container-grown clumps in spring in partial to full shade with consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Heliconia angusta is one of the more shade-tolerant and cold-tolerant heliconias, growing naturally as a forest understory plant and surviving temperatures to 28–30°F with rhizome protection in zone 9. In the Northern Hemisphere it blooms November–January ('Christmas Heliconia'). Divide clumps every 3–4 years. Excellent as a long-lasting cut flower (2–3 weeks). Propagate by dividing rhizomes.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Christmas Heliconia is one of the most shade-tolerant of the commonly grown heliconias, naturally adapted to forest understory conditions and producing reliable bloom even in relatively dim positions in zone 9-11 shade gardens. Heliconia angusta is a compact, clump-forming, rhizomatous tropical perennial native to Brazil, Bolivia, and Peru in the Heliconiaceae family, one of the smaller-growing heliconias and one of the most valuable for several reasons: its relatively compact size (3–6 feet, more manageable than the massive H. bihai or H. caribaea), its notable shade tolerance, its moderate cold tolerance for a heliconia (surviving zone 9 winters), and its bloom season — in the Northern Hemisphere, H. angusta produces its striking inflorescences from November through January, coinciding with the Christmas holiday season and earning the affectionate name 'Christmas Heliconia.' The erect inflorescences bear bright red, narrow, keeled bracts alternating along the stem in the classic heliconia 'lobster claw' pattern, with small white to pale yellow flowers emerging from within each bract. The narrow bracts (the 'angusta' — narrow) give the inflorescence a slimmer, more elegant silhouette compared to broader-bracted species. An excellent choice for zone 9 shade gardens where larger, less cold-tolerant heliconias cannot be reliably grown.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Heliconia angusta for Florida shade gardens
LSU AgCenter — Cold-Hardy Heliconias for Gulf Coast Louisiana Zone 9
Heliconia Society International — Heliconia angusta: Christmas bloomer for shade gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Heliconia angusta for zone 9 Gulf Coast tropical gardens

common_name: Sexy Pink Heliconia (Chartacea Heliconia, Strawberry Cream Heliconia, Peach Heliconia)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or container-grown clumps in late spring in partial shade to full sun with consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained, fertile soil; Heliconia chartacea is a large, frost-sensitive species suited to zone 10-11 outdoor planting. This species grows to impressive dimensions — 6–15 feet tall — and its pendant inflorescences are among the most prized in the heliconia family for cut flower use, with vase lives of 3–4 weeks. The cultivar 'Sexy Pink' (pale pink and cream bracts) and 'Burle Marx' (deeper pink) are widely available. Provide ample space and consistent moisture.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in consistently moist, fertile, well-draining soil — Sexy Pink Heliconia grows most impressively with regular moisture and some shade protection from the most intense afternoon sun in zone 10 conditions. Heliconia chartacea is a large, clump-forming, rhizomatous tropical perennial native to Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Brazil in the Heliconiaceae family, producing what many heliconia enthusiasts consider the most beautiful and sophisticated inflorescences in the entire genus — inflorescences so striking that the most popular cultivar was given the flamboyant trade name 'Sexy Pink.' The inflorescences are large (18–36 inches long), pendant (hanging downward on arching peduncles), and composed of broadly boat-shaped, overlapping bracts in the most delicate coloration of any heliconia: soft, creamy pale pink to rosy pink, with a velvety or slightly glossy texture and a subtle color gradation from base to tip — the 'Sexy Pink' cultivar is perhaps the palest, most pastel, most refined of the pendant heliconias. The 'Burle Marx' cultivar (named for the famous Brazilian landscape architect Roberto Burle Marx) has deeper, richer pink to rose-pink bracts. The large, banana-like foliage provides an impressive tropical backdrop. In the cut flower industry, H. chartacea inflorescences command premium prices for their elegance and exceptional vase life (3–4 weeks). One of the finest pendant heliconias for zone 10-11 tropical gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Heliconia chartacea for Florida tropical landscapes
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Heliconia chartacea 'Sexy Pink' and 'Burle Marx'
Heliconia Society International — Heliconia chartacea cultivar guide
LSU AgCenter — Tropical Heliconias for Gulf Coast Louisiana Zone 10-11

common_name: Ulam Raja (King's Salad, Cosmos Herb, Wild Cosmos, Purple Cosmos Herb)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in full sun; Ulam Raja grows rapidly and begins producing edible young leaves and shoots within 6–8 weeks. It self-seeds prolifically and can naturalize as a beneficial weed in zone 9-11 gardens. Young leaves, stems, and flower buds are eaten raw in salads or as a vegetable throughout Southeast Asia — particularly popular in Malaysia, Indonesia, and Thailand as 'ulam' (raw herb eaten with meals). The leaves have a pleasantly sour, slightly bitter, herbal flavor and are high in antioxidants and nutrients. Pull plants when they become woody and reseed continuously.

directions: Direct sow in full sun in any well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Ulam Raja is a fast-growing, easy-care tropical annual that germinates rapidly and begins producing harvestable young leaves and shoot tips within 6–8 weeks of seeding. Cosmos caudatus is a tropical annual herb native to Mexico and Central America in the Asteraceae family that has become widely naturalized and deeply embedded in the food cultures of Southeast Asia — particularly Malaysia, Indonesia, Thailand, and the Philippines — where it is consumed daily as an 'ulam,' the term for raw herbs and vegetables eaten fresh alongside cooked rice meals. The young leaves, tender shoots, and flower buds are eaten raw in salads, combined with other 'ulam' herbs, or used as a garnish and vegetable in cooking — the flavor is pleasantly sour, slightly bitter, and distinctly herbal, reminiscent of a more complex watercress. Research by Malaysian institutions has documented exceptionally high levels of antioxidants, phenolic compounds, and nutrients in the young foliage, and traditional Malay medicine credits the plant with anti-aging properties that are reflected in its honorific common name 'Ulam Raja' — 'the king's herb/salad.' The plant also produces the characteristic fine-petaled pink to magenta flowers of the Cosmos genus throughout the warm months. Self-seeds abundantly and naturalizes in zone 9-11 gardens as a continuously productive edible green.
sources: University of Malaysia — Cosmos caudatus: nutritional composition and antioxidant properties
National University of Singapore — Ulam Raja: traditional Malay medicinal herb and food
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Edible tropical herbs for Gulf Coast zone 9-10 gardens
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cosmos caudatus as tropical edible herb
